skill.name: hook-development
skill.description: Guides users through building, testing, validating, and debugging Claude Code hook configurations for plugin and settings workflows.
skill.summary: Create Claude Code hooks for validation, automation, and workflow policy enforcement.
skill.author: davila7

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, filesystem, and sensitive-file patterns, but most are examples for detecting or blocking dangerous hook behavior rather than malicious actions. No prompt injection attempt or confirmed malicious intent was found. Publication is acceptable with warnings because one helper script constructs a shell command from user-supplied paths, and some documentation examples log full hook input that may contain secrets.

medium_findings.title: Shell command construction in hook test helper
medium_findings.description: The test helper builds a bash -c command using the provided hook script path. This is useful for testing hooks, but unsafe paths or arguments can alter the command that is executed. This is a real command injection risk in a local utility, not confirmed malicious intent.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The script places user-controlled hook script text into a bash -c string. The utility is intentionally executing hook scripts, but the shell construction increases injection risk.

medium_findings.title: Examples may log sensitive hook input
medium_findings.description: The advanced examples show logging raw hook input to a database and to an audit log. Hook input can include file paths, command text, prompts, and content previews, so copying these examples without redaction could store secrets.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples explicitly write the full hook input to external storage and a user log. The risk depends on user adoption, but the sensitive-data exposure path is clear.

medium_findings.title: Prompt hook examples can expose file content to model context
medium_findings.description: Several prompt hook examples include tool input or content previews in the prompt. This is normal for context-aware validation, but users should avoid sending secrets, credentials, or private data into prompt hooks without redaction.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited examples intentionally pass tool input and content preview text into prompts. The behavior is legitimate, but sensitive data exposure is plausible.

low_findings.title: Destructive command detections are protective examples
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged rm, dd, mkfs, and device-file patterns. In the reviewed context, these strings are used to deny dangerous Bash operations or test that a validation hook blocks them.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding code checks for dangerous commands and returns deny decisions. The examples are defensive rather than instructions to run destructive commands.

low_findings.title: Sensitive file and system path detections are guardrails
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged .env, system directories, and traversal strings. The reviewed examples use those strings to deny or require confirmation for risky file writes.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged paths appear inside validation conditions and prompt criteria that protect files. No evidence found that the skill reads or exfiltrates those files.

low_findings.title: External command usage is central to hook development
low_findings.description: Many static hits are shell snippets and command-hook examples. This is expected for a hook-development skill, but users should review generated hooks before enabling them.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The skill is explicitly about command and prompt hooks, so shell examples are expected. The risk is operational rather than evidence of malicious behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: User-controlled shell string execution
dangerous_patterns.description: The test helper executes a composed command through bash -c while including a user-provided hook script path. This pattern should be replaced with argument arrays or strict path validation.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The direct bash -c construction is visible and includes the hook path variable. Exploitability depends on how users pass hook paths.

dangerous_patterns.title: Raw hook input persistence
dangerous_patterns.description: Documentation examples persist complete hook input to a database or audit file. This can retain secrets if users copy the pattern without redaction.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The examples explicitly store the full input payload. Hook payloads often include command text and file content previews.

content.value_statement: Hook configuration is easy to break because events, matchers, exit codes, and prompt hooks have strict behavior. This skill provides patterns, scripts, and checks for building Claude Code hooks with clearer validation and testing.
actual_capabilities: Explains command hooks and prompt-based hooks for Claude Code events.
Shows plugin hooks.json and user settings hook formats.
Provides Bash examples for validating commands and file writes.
Includes scripts to lint hook scripts and validate hook configuration files.
Includes a test helper for running hooks with sample event payloads.
Documents migration patterns from Bash validation to prompt hooks.
limitations: Examples require careful review before use in sensitive repositories.
The helper scripts assume Bash, jq, and standard Unix tools are available.
Prompt hooks may expose tool input to model context unless users redact data.
The skill provides guidance and examples, not a complete managed hook runtime.

use_cases.title: Block risky tool operations
use_cases.description: Create PreToolUse hooks that ask for confirmation or deny dangerous Bash commands and sensitive file writes.

use_cases.title: Automate project setup
use_cases.description: Use SessionStart hooks to detect project type and set useful environment context for Claude Code sessions.

use_cases.title: Migrate validation logic
use_cases.description: Replace brittle shell-only checks with prompt hooks that evaluate intent, context, and edge cases.

prompt_templates.title: Create a basic hook
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a simple Claude Code PreToolUse hook that checks Write operations and asks before writing to sensitive files.

prompt_templates.title: Validate a hook configuration
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my hooks.json structure for Claude Code. Check event names, matchers, hook types, timeout values, and plugin format.

prompt_templates.title: Convert Bash checks
prompt_templates.prompt: Convert my Bash command validation hook into a prompt-based hook. Keep the same security goals and explain behavior changes.

prompt_templates.title: Design a hook policy
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a Claude Code hook policy for a team repository. Include PreToolUse, Stop, testing steps, failure behavior, and secret-handling rules.

output_examples.input: Create a hook that blocks dangerous shell commands.
output_examples.output: A PreToolUse hook plan with Bash matcher coverage, deny conditions for destructive commands, confirmation behavior for elevated privileges, and testing steps.

output_examples.input: Help me debug why my hook is not running.
output_examples.output: A checklist covering hook file location, plugin wrapper format, executable permissions, event matcher names, debug mode, and sample input testing.

output_examples.input: Move my file-write validation to a prompt hook.
output_examples.output: A prompt-hook design that evaluates system directories, credential files, traversal attempts, and content exposure before approving writes.

best_practices: Test hooks with sample inputs before enabling them in active projects.
Redact secrets before logging hook payloads or sending content to prompt hooks.
Use short timeouts and clear deny messages so hooks do not block workflows silently.
anti_patterns: Do not copy logging examples into production without filtering sensitive fields.
Do not build shell commands from untrusted paths or arguments.
Do not rely only on simple substring checks for destructive operations.

faq.question: What hook events does this skill cover?
faq.answer: It covers PreToolUse, PostToolUse, Stop, SubagentStop, SessionStart, SessionEnd, UserPromptSubmit, PreCompact, and Notification.

faq.question: Does this skill create runnable hook scripts?
faq.answer: It provides examples and helper scripts. Users should review and adapt them before enabling hooks.

faq.question: Can it help with prompt-based hooks?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains prompt hooks and provides migration patterns from Bash checks to natural language validation.

faq.question: What dependencies do the scripts need?
faq.answer: The scripts are Bash-based and commonly use jq, timeout, and standard Unix command-line tools.

faq.question: Is it safe for sensitive repositories?
faq.answer: Use it with caution. Redact hook input, avoid raw payload logging, and inspect any command hook before enabling it.

faq.question: Can it validate hooks.json files?
faq.answer: Yes. The included schema validator checks JSON syntax, hook types, matchers, event names, and timeout values.
